section.title: Greeting to Gaius
section.sectionRationale: The letter opens with the elder's warm address to Gaius and a prayer for his whole-life flourishing, then names the thing that gives the elder his greatest joy: that Gaius is walking in the truth. This sets the key that the whole letter is played in.
section.thesis: Real Christian love flows through the truth: the elder prays for Gaius body and soul, and rejoices most of all that his beloved friend is not merely holding the truth but walking in it.
section.frameNote: Two beats: the address and prayer for Gaius; the joy of hearing that he walks in the truth.

units.label: The address and prayer
units.note: the elder to his beloved friend

verses.text: The elder, To the {{agapetos|beloved}} Gaius, whom I love in {{aletheia|the truth}}:
Beloved, I pray that in every way you may prosper and enjoy good health, as your soul also prospers.

units.label: Joy over a life in the truth

verses.text: For I was overjoyed when the brothers came and testified about your devotion to the truth, in which you continue to {{peripateo|walk}}.
I have no greater {{chara|joy}} than to hear that my children are walking in the truth.

agapetos.gloss: Beloved, dearly loved. It is the elder's favourite word for Gaius, repeated in verses 1, 2, 5, and 11. The letter is soaked in warm affection; before a single instruction is given, Gaius is named as one who is loved, and loved in the truth.

aletheia.gloss: Truth, reality, what is genuine and trustworthy. In John's writings it is more than accurate ideas; it is the reality revealed in Christ, who is Himself the truth. The elder loves Gaius in the truth, and truth is the recurring drumbeat of this short letter.

peripateo.gloss: To walk, and by metaphor to conduct one's whole life. To walk in the truth means truth is not only believed but lived, step by ordinary step. The picture is of a settled, continuing way of life shaped by the gospel.

chara.gloss: Joy, gladness. The elder's deepest gladness is not in comfort or success but in seeing his spiritual children walk in the truth. It is a pastor's heart, measuring his happiness by the faithfulness of those he loves.

basic.context: This is the most personal letter in the New Testament, a short note from an old church leader, the elder, to a friend named Gaius. He opens with love and a prayer that Gaius would do well in every part of life, body and soul. Then he tells Gaius what makes him happiest of all: travelling believers have reported that Gaius is living out the truth.
basic.hermeneutics: It is a real letter to a real friend, so we read it as warm, practical wisdom for church life, not as a set of rules. The abiding point shines through: the elder measures a good life not by health or wealth alone but by whether someone is walking in the truth, actually living what they believe.
basic.originalLanguages: The word beloved, agapetos, sets the tone. Truth, aletheia, is the drumbeat of the whole letter. And walking, peripateo, means a whole way of life, not just holding right ideas. The elder's joy, chara, is tied entirely to his friends living in the truth.
basic.typology: Gaius walking in the truth is a small picture of every faithful believer who follows Jesus, the one who is the way, the truth, and the life. The elder's fatherly joy over his children echoes God's own delight in His children who walk in His ways.
basic.christ: The truth John loves is finally a person: Jesus, who called Himself the truth. To walk in the truth is to walk with Christ and to live the way He lived. The whole letter assumes Him as the centre and standard.
basic.god: God cares about the whole person, so the elder can pray for Gaius to prosper and be in good health as his soul prospers. And God is honoured when His people do not just know the truth but live it out day by day.
basic.crossReferences: 2 John is a companion letter with the same love of truth. John's Gospel records Jesus saying He is the truth. And Jesus teaches that we show our love for Him by keeping His word, which is walking in the truth.
basic.oneStory: The Bible's story moves toward a people who know God and live in His truth. This little greeting shows that story landing in one friendship: an old shepherd rejoicing that his friend Gaius is walking, step by step, in the truth revealed in Christ.

context.literary: The opening of a brief personal letter, following the ancient form of sender, recipient, and a health wish, but filled with Christian content. The prayer for prosperity and health frames the real concern, the prospering of the soul, and verses 3 and 4 introduce the theme of truth that governs everything that follows.
context.historical: The writer calls himself simply the elder, by long tradition the apostle John late in life at Ephesus. Gaius is a beloved member of a nearby church. Letters like this were carried by travelling teachers, the very people who become the subject of the next section, so the warm opening is not merely polite but sets up the letter's business.
context.canonical: One of three short letters of John, closely tied to 2 John, and standing near the end of the New Testament as a window into everyday church life: hospitality, truth, leadership, and love among ordinary believers.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To assure Gaius of the elder's love, to pray for his complete well-being, and above all to express joy that Gaius is walking in the truth, thereby setting the standard against which the rest of the letter, including the conflict with Diotrephes, will be measured.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The greeting describes the elder's affection and prayer; it does not command anything yet. The abiding principle it models is that mature Christian love delights most in the spiritual faithfulness of others and prays for their whole-life good.

debates.question: Does the prayer to prosper and enjoy good health support a prosperity gospel?

views.label: A normal, warm health wish
views.case: The phrase follows a common letter-opening custom of wishing a friend well; it is genuine care for Gaius's body and circumstances, not a promise that faith guarantees wealth or healing.

views.label: A soul-first prayer
views.case: The measure is set by as your soul also prospers; the elder can pray for outward good precisely because Gaius's soul is already flourishing, which reorders, rather than inflates, material concern.

debates.bearing: Either way the verse cannot be pressed into a prosperity gospel; the controlling wish is a healthy soul, and the letter's joy is over walking in the truth, not over riches.

study.originalLanguages: The greeting is built on a few warm words. Gaius is agapetos, beloved, a term repeated through the letter. He is loved in aletheia, the truth, John's great theme, which is finally the reality revealed in Christ. The elder rejoices that Gaius continues to peripateo, to walk, in that truth, a verb for one's whole manner of life, not merely right belief. And his chara, his joy, has no rival: nothing gladdens this old shepherd more than to hear that his children are walking in the truth.

typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in following Christ, who is the truth, so that the believer's daily walk is a life shaped by and shared with Him.

typology.type: The elder's fatherly joy over his children
typology.fulfillment: Reflects God the Father's own delight in children who walk in His ways, and points to the joy of the great Shepherd over His flock.

study.christ: The truth that saturates this greeting is not an abstraction; John's Gospel records Jesus saying, I am the way and the truth and the life. To be loved in the truth and to walk in the truth is therefore to be bound to Christ Himself and to live as He lived. The elder's joy over faithful children mirrors the joy of Jesus, the good Shepherd, who knows His own and rejoices over every one who follows Him.
study.god: God is shown to care for the whole person: the elder can pray for Gaius to prosper and be healthy even as he prizes the soul above all. God is honoured not merely when truth is believed but when it is walked, lived out in ordinary steps. And the fatherly joy of the elder reflects a God who delights in His children and measures blessing by faithfulness, not by outward success.

crossReferences.note: The elder uses almost the same words, rejoicing to find children walking in the truth, tying the two letters closely together.
Jesus declares Himself the truth, so that walking in the truth is walking with Him.
To hold to Jesus' teaching is to be truly His disciples, and the truth sets us free.
Walking in the light, in step with the truth, marks genuine fellowship with God.

study.oneStory: Scripture is moving toward a people who know God truly and live in His truth. Here that grand story lands in a single friendship: an aged pastor, likely the apostle John, pours out love for Gaius and finds his highest joy in one plain report, that his friend keeps walking in the truth. The whole letter, and the whole Bible, is about lives increasingly shaped by the truth that is Christ.

sermon.title: Walk in the Truth, Part One
sermon.why: From Begg's short series on 3 John, this message opens the letter, drawing out the elder's love for Gaius, the prayer for his prospering soul, and the joy of walking in the truth. A clear, orthodox handling of this greeting.

quiz.q: What gives the elder his greatest joy?

options.text: Hearing that his children are walking in the truth, living it out, not just believing it.
options.note: Verse 4 names this as his greatest joy, and walking means a whole way of life.

options.text: Hearing that Gaius is healthy and prosperous.
options.note: He does pray for this, but his greatest joy is Gaius walking in the truth.
The reports gladden him, but the joy is in what they report: a life in the truth.
His joy is entirely in his children's faithfulness, not in his own status.

quiz.q: What does it mean that Gaius walks in the truth?

options.text: His whole way of life is shaped by the truth revealed in Christ, lived out step by step.
options.note: The verb walk pictures a continuing manner of life, not mere agreement.

options.text: He believes all the correct doctrines.
options.note: Right belief is included, but walking means living it, not only holding it.
The travellers are others; walking in the truth describes Gaius's daily life.

options.text: He always wins arguments about the faith.
options.note: The point is a faithful life, not skill in debate.

quiz.q: How should we understand the prayer that Gaius may prosper and enjoy good health?

options.text: A warm, whole-person prayer, measured by the prospering of his soul.
options.note: The soul sets the standard: as your soul also prospers.

options.text: A customary friendly health wish shaped by Christian care.
options.note: True to the form of the day, though it understates the soul-first measure.

options.text: A promise that faith always brings wealth and healing.
options.note: This misreads a personal prayer as a guarantee; it is not a prosperity promise.

options.text: A sign that the elder cared only about material things.
options.note: He prizes the soul above all; the body is prayed for within that.

quiz.q: Who is the truth that runs through this letter, ultimately?

options.text: Jesus Christ, who said He is the way, the truth, and the life.
options.note: In John, truth is finally the reality revealed in Christ Himself.

options.text: The body of correct Christian teaching.
options.note: Sound teaching is included, but it centres on the person of Christ.
The truth is not private opinion but the revealed reality in Jesus.
Honesty matters, but that is not the deep meaning of truth here.

questions: The elder's greatest joy was hearing that his friend walked in the truth. What brings you your greatest joy, and how does that compare?
Walking in the truth means living it, not just believing it. Where is there a gap between what you say you believe and how you actually live?
The elder prayed for Gaius's whole life, body and soul, with the soul as the measure. How would your prayers change if your soul's health set the standard?
Gaius was loved in the truth by an old shepherd who prayed for him. Who has loved and prayed you toward Christ, and have you thanked them?
If travelling believers reported on your life this week, what would they say about the way you are walking?

translationNotes.note: The elder, ho presbyteros, is by long tradition the apostle John in his old age; the same self-title opens 2 John. In the truth can describe both the sphere of the elder's love and its genuineness.
Prosper renders a verb meaning to go well or have a good journey; combined with the health wish it follows a common letter-opening custom, here reshaped around the prospering of the soul.
My children is the affectionate language of a spiritual father for those he has helped to faith or discipled, as also in 1 John.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 3 John 1:1-4. The BSB combines the closing greeting into verse 14, so the book ends at 1:14.
